name: Follow Up on a Stalled Claim Past the Statutory Acknowledgment Window
description: Re-engage an adjuster who has gone silent past their state's statutory acknowledgment / response window. Document the silence, invoke the regulation by name, and create a paper trail that supports a department-of-insurance complaint or bad-faith escalation if the stall continues.
disclaimer: Agent must NEVER admit fault, NEVER agree to a settlement on the operator's behalf, NEVER speculate about prior conditions, NEVER sign a release. All of those route through ask_operator.

context.when_to_use: A claim has been filed, but the adjuster has not acknowledged, requested information, or made a coverage decision within the state-mandated window (commonly 10-15 days for acknowledgment, 30-40 days for a decision once proof-of-loss is submitted). Use AFTER `file-fnol-auto-claim` and BEFORE `dispute-denied-claim`.
preconditions: Claim number and assigned adjuster name are known.
User knows their state's statutory window (or you've looked it up — most states publish 'Unfair Claims Settlement Practices' regulations).
User has copies of every prior communication (email, voicemail, certified mail receipts).

principles: The regulation is your leverage. Naming the specific statute (e.g. 'California Insurance Code §790.03' or 'New York Reg 64') signals you're not a casual caller.
Document everything. Every voicemail, every dead-end transfer, every promise of a callback — log the date, time, and rep name.
Be firm, not angry. The goal is action, not catharsis. Angry calls get logged as 'difficult customer' and slow-walked.
Escalate by structure: adjuster → supervisor → claims manager → consumer affairs → department of insurance. Each step is a separate ask.
Silence is a tactic carriers use; certified mail and DOI complaints are the counter-tactics.

phrases.opener: Hi, I'm calling about claim [number], opened [date]. I have not received [acknowledgment / a coverage decision / a response to my [date] inquiry], and the [statutory window] has now passed. I'd like to understand the current status and the next concrete action with a date.
phrases.invoke_statute: Under [state] regulations on unfair claims settlement practices, the carrier owes [acknowledgment within X days / a decision within Y days of proof of loss]. We are past that window. I want to confirm the carrier's position on that timeline.
phrases.request_supervisor: Okay — could I also be transferred to the claims supervisor for [adjuster name]? Not because you've done anything wrong; I just want this visible at the next level given the timeline.
phrases.request_written_status: Please send a written status update by end of business today, including: current claim status, what specifically is outstanding, who is responsible, and the date the carrier expects to resolve it.
phrases.doi_warning: If we can't get a concrete next step with a date today, my next step will be filing a complaint with the [state] Department of Insurance. I'd rather resolve this with you — what's possible?
phrases.log_callback: Could I get your full name, your direct extension, and a reference number for this call?
phrases.graceful_close: Thank you. To confirm: next action is [action], owner is [name], target date is [date], and a written status update will arrive by [time].

tactics.name: Open with the timeline, not the complaint
tactics.script: Use the `opener` phrase. State the FNOL date, the regulation's deadline, and that the window has passed. Don't editorialise — let the dates speak.

tactics.name: Name the statute
tactics.when: Rep says 'we're processing' without a date.
tactics.script: Use the `invoke_statute` phrase. If you have looked up the specific code section, name it. Even 'your state's unfair claims settlement practices act' is meaningfully better than nothing.

tactics.name: Ask for the supervisor
tactics.when: Adjuster offers no concrete next step or claims they're 'waiting on the file'.
tactics.script: Use the `request_supervisor` phrase. Frame it as not blaming the adjuster. Carriers route 'supervisor requested + statute named' calls differently from standard inquiries.

tactics.name: Demand a written status update with a deadline
tactics.when: Any rep on the call agrees there will be 'follow-up'.
tactics.script: Use the `request_written_status` phrase. Verbal promises don't survive adjuster turnover. Written promises survive and become evidence.

tactics.name: Warn about a DOI complaint
tactics.when: Supervisor still offers no concrete date.
tactics.script: Use the `doi_warning` phrase. State it factually, not as a threat — 'next step is filing with the DOI' is information, not anger. Carriers genuinely move faster when DOI is on the table because DOI complaints damage their market-conduct exam scores.

tactics.name: Log everything
tactics.script: Use the `log_callback` phrase before ending. Capture the rep's full name, extension, the call's reference number, and the exact promises made. After the call, email a recap to the adjuster restating what was agreed — that converts verbal into written.

boundaries: Do NOT accept 'I'll check and call you back' without a specific date AND a written confirmation commitment.
Do NOT use the DOI warning as a bluff — only mention it if the operator has actually authorised filing the complaint on the agreed timeline.
Do NOT settle for being transferred into a voicemail. If transferred, get the supervisor's direct line first.
Do NOT agree to provide additional documentation that has already been provided — push back that it's on file.
Do NOT lose your temper. Document and escalate; the paper trail wins.
success_signals: Adjuster or supervisor commits to a specific next action with a specific date.
Written status update is promised by end of business.
Carrier acknowledges the statutory window has been missed and offers a remedy (rental extension, expedited inspection, etc.).
Rep provides a direct extension and reference number for the call.
failure_signals: Three escalation levels (adjuster, supervisor, claims manager) all decline to commit to a date.
Carrier asks for documents already provided — stall tactic.
Call disconnects 'accidentally' and no callback arrives within 30 minutes.

exit_strategy.on_success: Read back the agreed action, owner, and date. Confirm the written status update timeline. Thank the rep and end. Immediately email a written recap to the adjuster + supervisor restating what was agreed.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Document the failure to commit. Route to `ask_operator` with the recommendation to file a DOI complaint and/or send a certified-mail demand letter citing the statute.
follow_ups: Email a recap of the call to the adjuster and supervisor within 1 hour.
Calendar the promised written status update deadline; trigger another follow-up call if it slips.
If no written update arrives, escalate to DOI complaint via `ask_operator`.

required_user_info: Claim number, adjuster name, original FNOL date
State of the claim (drives which statute applies)
Log of all prior communications with dates
Whether operator authorises DOI complaint as next step if call fails
